summary: Opinion blog by Carole Herder urging horse owners to question barn/trainer agendas and follow their own path rather than conform to the dominant show-ring methodology.
Carole Herder essay arguing horses don't fake lameness or scheme like people, and that owners should accept a horse's behavior as honest signals of discomfort or fear.
Carole Herder explains predator vs prey dynamics, noting humans' forward-facing eyes read as predatory, and how to adjust behavior to make horses feel safe.
Carole Herder advocates incorporating play and at-liberty interaction into the horse relationship, referencing Dr. Bruce Nock's book and ideas like large soccer-style balls.
Short Carole Herder post highlighting an Eventing Nation article on barefoot eventing, quoting Bridget Brewer on barefoot challenges and positioning Cavallo hoof boots as support.
Carole Herder's personal anecdote about a paddock-paradise track system, soft vs hard ground affecting hoof health, and her husband's tractor help spreading rock.
Part 2 of Carole Herder's predator-and-prey series, advising owners to zig-zag rather than walk directly at horses and explaining Monty Roberts' join-up technique.
Carole Herder defines and differentiates three common hoof conditions — laminitis, founder, and navicular — covering symptoms, causes, prevention, and care.
Carole Herder discusses equine nutrition, noting diet affects horses individually and recounting how changing one horse's feed harmed its feet and condition before recovery.
Carole Herder encourages learning basic horse anatomy terms (coronary band, hock, stifle, pastern) for better vet/trainer communication, linking to learning tools and a 3D app.
Reflective Carole Herder essay on why she loves horses — their nobility, beauty, and spirit — and why she opposes horseshoes in favor of a natural, barefoot life.
Carole Herder describes the joys of trail riding across summer and frosty winter days, the anticipation of prepping gear, and bonding with her horse outdoors.
